macro_segments.title: Summary of Prior Debate: Rabbi Yehuda vs. Rabbi Meir on Individual Sacrifices at the Central Bamah

micro_segments.title: Review of Rabbi Yehuda's permissive position on private bamot and public bamah
Review of Rabbi Meir and Chachamim's restrictive position limiting individuals to olah and shelamim
Explanation of why bamot were permissible: absence of the Aron made Gilgal a bamah gedolah, not a true Mikdash
Theological principle: restriction to one central location only when God's presence is particular there

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Ultra-Restrictive Model: Bamah Gedolah as Extended Bamah Ketanah

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's position that even the public bamah allows only Korban Pesach and fixed obligations
Derivation from the verse about Pesach in Gilgal and conceptual foundation on private bamah
Gemara's difficulty: if based on private bamah, how can obligatory sacrifices be permitted at public bamah
Resolution: only obligatory olot, not chatas, connect to private bamah model

macro_segments.title: Transition to Shilo: When the Mishkan Became a Permanent Resting Place

micro_segments.title: Source for the structure of the Mishkan in Shilo: combination of stone foundation (beis) and cloth roof (ohel)
Shilo as menucha (temporary resting place) rather than nachala (permanent inheritance)
Restrictions on eating kodashim: connected to seeing the Mikdash, not defined by city of Shilo

macro_segments.title: The Line of Sight Principle: Eating Kodashim from Anywhere You See Shilo

micro_segments.title: Verse on restrictions against bringing sacrifices everywhere once central place exists
Midrashic interpretations from Yosef's virtue enabling eating from distant locations
Baraita on visibility: must see Shilo without obstruction, but need not see it completely
Rav Pappa's refined formulation: even partial sight of Shilo suffices
Difficult cases: standing and seeing versus sitting and not seeing

macro_segments.title: Divine Presence in Three Tribal Locations: Benjamin's Theological Centrality

micro_segments.title: Teaching of Rav Dimi: Shechinah dwelt in three places, all within Benjamin's portion
Apparent contradiction: Shilo in Yosef/Ephraim but claimed to be in Benjamin
Rabbi Yochanan's alternative: Shechinah hovers over Benjamin perpetually in this world
Tannaitic dispute on Benjamin/Shilo: first Tannaim limit divine presence to Mikdash periods; Rabbi holds it was always in Benjamin

macro_segments.title: Historical Chronology: Computing the Years Shilo Stood via Subtraction from the 480-Year Count

micro_segments.title: Framework: 480 years from Exodus to Mikdash construction; subtract known periods to find Shilo years
Final calculation: 480 minus 111 (40+14+57) equals 369 years for Shilo
Wilderness period: 39 years (Mishkan built year 2, redeemed year 40)
Gilgal period: 14 years (7 conquest + 7 division, derived from Calev's age)
Nov and Givon period: 57 years (calculation from Eli's death through Shmuel, Shaul, David to Solomon year 4)
Derivation of Nov period from Eli's death at Aron capture; Shilo's destruction ends that era
Aron's return and 20-year stay in Kiryat Ye'arim: breakdown among Shmuel, Shaul, David in Hevron
David's 40 years (7 in Hevron, 33 in Jerusalem) plus Solomon's initial 4 years yields 57 total

topical_tags.term: Nov and Givon (locations after Shilo destruction)
Line of sight principle (seeing the Mikdash)
Rabbi Yochanan (on red heifer sprinkling for erel)
Rav Dimi (on three locations of Shechinah)
Eli the Priest (death marking Shilo's fall)
Kodashim Kalim (lesser holy things, eaten widely)
Kodashim Kedoshim (most holy things, eaten in courtyard)
